channel.title: Nature Communications
channel.description:
Nature Communications is an open access, multidisciplinary journal dedicated to publishing high-quality research in all areas of the biological, health, physical, chemical and Earth sciences. Papers published by the journal aim to represent important advances of significance to specialists within each field.

We are committed to providing an efficient service for both authors and readers. Our team of independent editors make rapid and fair publication decisions. Prompt dissemination of accepted papers to a wide readership and beyond is achieved through a programme of continuous online publication.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: Spin-polarized edge modes between different magnet-superconductor-hybrids
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 11 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-71687-3

Combining magnetic materials with superconductors in heterostructures is a promising pathway for realizing topological superconductivity. Here, Zahner et al show that the boundary formed between monolayer and bilayer manganese deposited on top of superconducting Tantalum can host spin polarized edge modes, the result of different topological superconducting phases of the monolayer and bilayer regions.
item.dc:title: Spin-polarized edge modes between different magnet-superconductor-hybrids
item.dc:creator: Felix Zahner
Felix Nickel
Roberto Lo Conte
Tim Drevelow
Roland Wiesendanger
Stefan Heinze
Kirsten von Bergmann

item.title: Mach-Zehnder atom interferometry with non-interacting trapped Bose-Einstein condensates
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 11 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-69692-7

Current implementations of trapped atom interferometers are limited by the detrimental role of atomic interactions and trapping potential imperfections. To address these challenges, the authors develop a Mach-Zender atom interferometer by means of a double-well potential array, enabling trapped-atom-based gradiometry.
item.dc:title: Mach-Zehnder atom interferometry with non-interacting trapped Bose-Einstein condensates
item.dc:creator: T. Petrucciani
A. Santoni
C. Mazzinghi
D. Trypogeorgos
F. S. Cataliotti
M. Inguscio
G. Modugno
A. Smerzi
L. Pezzè
M. Fattori
